data.title: Singapore's Budget Expected to Be Positive for Equities — Market Talk
data.description: J.P. Morgan Securities maintains its positive view on Singapore equities after the country's annual budget. The government will provide an S$1.5 billion additional top-up to the Financial Sector Development Fund. The government has also raised its fiscal year 2025 surplus estimate to more than double the earlier projection, it notes. The private sector's growth should be driven by factors, including positive surprises in the fiscal buffer and a strong macro backdrop. "We believe these tailwinds, together with an extension of financial market support, should drive Singapore equities [Straits Times Index] closer to our 6000 target," analysts write in a report. The STI is 1.0% lower at 4968.74.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

data.title: Maxis Outlook Faces 5G Cost Structure Risks — Market Talk
data.description: Maxis's earnings outlook appears cautious, Kenanga IB analyst Kylie Chan Sze Zan says in a note. Earnings risks could emerge under an evolving 5G cost structure, given its relatively smaller scale, she says. With a subscriber base about 50% smaller than its key competitor, Maxis may face greater pressure if it shifts from usage-based 5G access charges to fixed minimum fees payable to Digital Nasional, a model already adopted by larger peers, she notes. A fixed-cost structure could limit its ability to absorb expenses efficiently due to lower traffic volumes, she adds. However, Chan raises Maxis's 2026 profit forecast by 8% on expectations of stronger net subscriber growth and improved revenue. Kenanga raises Maxis's target price to MYR3.63 from MYR3.56, while maintaining a market perform rating on the stock. Shares are 3.1% higher at MYR3.95. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: Chinese Home Prices Continue to Fall in January
data.description:
Home prices in major Chinese cities extended their decline in January, as the world's second-largest economy continued to grapple with a prolonged property slump.

Home prices in China's 70 biggest cities fell 3.3% in January from a year earlier, widening from a 3.0% drop in December, according to calculations by The Wall Street Journal based on data released by the National Bureau of Statistics on Friday. Of the 70 cities, 65 reported a year-on-year decline last month, the same as in December.

Meanwhile, when compared with the prior month, home prices in those cities dropped 0.37% in January, compared with a 0.37% fall recorded in December. For January, there were 62 cities reporting month-on-month price declines, compared with 65 in the prior month.

Write to Singapore editors at singaporeeditors.dowjones.com

data.title: China New Home Prices Drop the Least in 7 Months
data.description:
China’s new home prices across 70 cities fell 3.1% year-on-year in January 2026, deepening from a 2.7% decline in December.

The latest data marked the 31st straight month of contraction and the sharpest drop since June, underscoring Beijing’s ongoing struggle to stabilize the property sector.

Among major cities, price declines widened in Guangzhou (-5.3% vs -4.8% in December), Shenzhen (-4.9% vs -4.4%), Chongqing (-3.5% vs -2.9%), and Tianjin (-4.0% vs -3.0%).

In Beijing, prices remained weak and unchanged in pace (-2.4% vs -2.4%).

Meanwhile, Shanghai still posted growth, though the increase softened (4.2% vs 4.8%), suggesting momentum in the top-tier market is also moderating.

data.title: China's new home prices extend decline in January
data.description:
China's new home prices extended their decline in January, official data showed on Friday, indicating weak demand that would further weigh on the country's cash-strapped developers.

Prices fell 0.4% month-on-month, matching the previous month's decline, according to Reuters calculations based on National Bureau of Statistics data.

On an annual basis, prices dropped 3.1% in January, quickening from a 2.7% fall in the previous month and marking the steepest decline in seven months.
